findings.description: The PDF has a structured layout with a central sale tag diagram surrounded by annotation callouts with arrows pointing to specific parts of the tag. The HTML renders everything as a flat, linear document without any of this spatial layout.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a bordered 'SALE TAG' box in the center with labeled arrows pointing from margin annotations (e.g., 'Harvest Date; may be mo/dy/yr...', '90 Day tag retention notice', etc.) to specific fields on the tag. HTML renders all content as a simple top-to-bottom text flow with no box, no arrows, and no spatial relationship between annotations and tag fields.

findings.description: The PDF contains a detailed diagram/graphic showing a shellstock sale tag with border, arrows, and annotation callouts. This entire graphic element is missing from the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF has a bordered rectangular tag diagram with directional arrows connecting explanatory text to specific tag fields. HTML has no image, diagram, or visual representation of the tag structure.

findings.description: In the PDF, the annotations/explanatory notes around the tag are visually distinct from the tag content itself (positioned outside the box as callouts). In the HTML, these explanatory notes are rendered in italics inline with the tag content, but the visual distinction between 'what's on the tag' and 'what's an explanation about the tag' is reduced.
findings.evidence: PDF clearly separates tag content (inside box) from explanatory annotations (outside box with arrows). HTML intermixes them sequentially with only italic formatting to differentiate.

findings.description: Nested <main> elements in the HTML. The outer <main> from the template wraps an inner <main> generated by the conversion, which is invalid HTML structure but does not affect content fidelity.
findings.evidence: HTML has <body><main>...<main>...</main>...</main></body> with two nested main elements.
